summary: My mother came from a place called Matsuo in Iwate. She did not have much education and worked hard in the fields like the rest of us. Growing up in that rural area shaped much of my early life and values. Although I was born elsewhere, my mother's roots in Matsuo were an important part of my family background.

I came to America when I was 21 years old in 1916 through a picture marriage. I thought America was a land of opportunity where I could make money and wear fine clothes, but it was much harder than I expected. Life here was difficult, especially working long days on the strawberry ranch in San Jose, and I often felt homesick and punished for leaving Japan against my parents' wishes. Despite the challenges, I stayed and worked hard to adapt to my new life.

My husband, Mr. Sakaguchi, was a year older than me and lived in San Jose when I arrived in America. We were matched through a picture marriage arranged by his grandmother who lived near my home in Japan. Though he was once a 'bad boy' as my father warned, I found him to be a hardworking man with a sunburned face from working outdoors on his strawberry ranch. Our marriage was traditional and strict, but his family welcomed me warmly when I arrived.

When I arrived in America, I came through Angel Island. I remember wondering which man was my husband because we had never met in person before. I heard him call my name, and that was how we found each other. I also heard stories of other picture brides who were disappointed when their husbands looked very different from their photos, and some even returned to Japan from Angel Island without marrying.

San Jose was where my husband had a strawberry ranch when I came to America in 1916. We lived in a prune ranch house surrounded by woods, which surprised me at first. Life there was tough because I had to work long hours pulling weeds between the strawberries, and I was slow at first compared to others. Despite the hardships, I eventually got used to the work and became better at it, though my husband was often impatient with me.

In San Jose, we took the strawberries we harvested to the Farmers' Association for shipping. Everyone there worked quickly and efficiently because they were used to the work, but I was slow and felt ashamed of myself. My husband often scolded me to hurry up because we had to ship the berries on time. The Farmers' Association was an important part of our farming life and the local community.
